label: Start
title: The Reasons for the Convention
summary:
When the colonies declared victory in the Revolutionary War in 1783, they created the Articles of Confederation as their founding document. The Articles described how the government of the new nation would be structured and managed. Four years later in 1787, issues with the Articles were starting to show.


Because many feared that a strong central government would function essentially like the King they had just fought to escape from, the Articles made the new government intentionally weak. But over the four years leading up to the Constitutional Convention, many began to suspect that the government was in fact too weak. It could pass laws, but had no power to enforce them. It had no ability to raise taxes, making it difficult to do much of anything.


In 1786, the government faced an armed uprising of debt-ridden Massachusetts farmers and had no ability to fight back — the states had to manage on their own. For many, this was a sign that the Articles were not a good basis for government.


Delegates from each state agreed to meet on May 14 to decide how the Articles could be updated to better handle the needs of government.
